title: practical english usage - Part 357
content:
Already is used to say that something has happened earlier than expected, 4 already The pears are not yet ripe, (more formal) Don't eat the pears - they aren’t ripe yet.

in a formal style.

Yet usually goes at the end of a clause, but it can go immediately after not Is supper ready yet? Has the mail come yet?

In questions, we  use yet to ask whether something expected has happened.

7s Sophie here?* ‘ Not yet.’  The mail hasn't come yet.

(but we  think that it will).

Not yet is used to say that something which  is expected has not happened 3 yet Still usually goes with the verb, in 'mid-position' (►  200).

You’re not still seeing that Jackson boy, are you?

I’ve been thinking for hours, but I still can’t decide.

vocabulary •  596  such It was a pleasure to meet such interesting people.

I'm sorry you had such a bad journey. (= You had a very bad journey, such is common before adjective + noun.

Another use of such is to talk about a high degree of some quality. In this sense, 3 high    degree not He's got an old Rolls-Royce. I'd like a car like that, (     I'd like suck & ea*) ... systems like this are called...

that kind of.

In an informal style, and with concrete nouns, we  prefer like this/that or this/ There are various forms of secret writing. Such systems are called 'codes'.

The committee wishes to raise fees. I would oppose such a decision.

in a formal style, with abstract nouns.

Such can mean Tike this/that', 'of this/that kind'. This is most common 2 'of  this/that      kind' not such a decision (     a such decision) such people such interesting ideas Such is used with nouns and noun phrases. It comes before a/an.

For still as a discourse marker meaning 'however', ►    30 1 .2.

‘ Are you all ready? ‘ No, Dan isn't.' ‘ When's Megan coming? ‘ She's already arrived.' Compare: All ready is not the same as already: it simply means the same as all + ready.

We have yet to hear from the bank. (= We   are still waiting to hear . ..) used in affirmative sentences in a formal style to mean 'still'.

Yet is normally used in questions and negative sentences. But it is sometimes 9 yet    meaning       'still' embarrassing, because her friends had already arrived.

I went to see if she had woken up yet, but she was still asleep. This was All three words can be related to a past moment instead of to the present.

8  related      to  a  past    moment She (has) already left. (AmE) - She has already left. (BrE) Have you paid / Did you pay yet? (AmE) - Have you paid yet? (BrE) Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary' •  597  such and so not I've never met such a nice person, (     .. .-asueh/se-nie She's such a baby.

Such comes before a!an.

rrtlr/v trttttr. . . j It was such good milk that we couldn 't stop drinking it.

They’re such fools, (     They’re sofbols;) We   use such before a noun (with or without an adjective).

1 such     before     (adjective      +) noun 597 such     and   so (= ... one or other famous person.) She's always telling you that she's met such-and-such a famous person.

Note this informal expression.

My doctor told me to avoid fatty foods such as bacon or hamburgers.

Such as is used to introduce examples.

(Less formal:. .. such a loud noise that it woke.. .) It was such a loud noise as to wake everybody in the house.

This is formal and not very common.

There is also a structure with such followed by ... + as to + infinitive.

It was such a cold afternoon that we stopped playing, (         was a very eeld use such .. . that.

Structures with very cannot be followed directly by thɑ/-elauses. Instead, we  can 5 such     ... that;    such     ...  as   to She has such a marvellous voice! He's such an idiot!

especially in exclamations.

However, in a very informal style such can also be used to mean ‘ very’  or ‘ great’ , Why was there such confusion?

- There was great confusion / wasn 't expecting such cold weather.

- The weather was very cold.

Why did you have such a bad day? (The information is already known.) - Tve had a very bad day. (giving information) Compare: (= like this/that) normally refers to information that is already known.
